rationale: Both mentions refer to the gradient of a scalar-valued function, a fundamental concept in multivariable calculus. Evidence shows the gradient is defined in lecture 1 and used in the chain rule in lecture 2, confirming the same object.

evidence_spans.span: Let \(f:\mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}\) be a differentiable scalar-valued function. A partial derivative measures how \(f\) changes when one coordinate changes while the other coordinates are held fixed. The gradient \(\nabla f(x)\) collects these partial derivatives into a vector. For a unit direction \(u\), the directional derivative is \(\nabla f(x)\cdot u\), so the gradient points in the direction of steepest local increase. A point where the gradient is the zero vector is called a stationary point.

Let \(g:\mathbb{R}^m \to \mathbb{R}^n\) and \(f:\mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}\) be differentiable functions. The composite function \(h = f \circ g\) maps an input \(x \in \mathbb{R}^m\) to \(f(g(x))\). The chain rule describes how the derivative of the composite function depends on the derivatives of \(f\) and \(g\).
